content.full_text:
July 8, 1991


James R. Sutton, Esq.
NIELSEN, MERKSAMER, HODGSON,
PARRINELLO & MUELLER
650 California Street, Suite 2650
San Francisco, CA 94108

                                        Re:  Your Request for Confirmation of
                                             Telephone Advice
                                             Our File No. I-91-277

Dear Mr. Sutton:

     This is in response to your request for confirmation of
advice rendered by telephone on May 15, 1991, in connection with
the honorarium provisions of the Political Reform Act.1 Since the
information that you originally requested in our telephone
conversation was general in nature, your subsequent letter asking
for confirmation of advice is considered to be a request for
informal assistance.2

     During our telephone conversation, I advised you that local
elected officials may receive payments for actual travel costs
made in connection with a speech, whether within or outside
California, and that those payments for travel costs are not
included in the limits on honoraria or gifts imposed by Section
89501.

     I also advised you that a travel reimbursement schedule is
currently being developed by the Technical Assistance and Analysis

_____________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-91-277
Page 2


Division of the Fair Political Practices Commission; payments for
travel may be reported on this schedule.

There appears to be one area of confusion in connection with
our telephone conversation. Any payment made for a local
official's travel costs under Section 89501 is presumed to be a
gift for purposes of disqualification in the context of conflict
of interest. (Sections 87100-87103.5.) Thus, although a travel
payment to a local official is not subject to a gift limit and may
be listed on a travel schedule in the official's statement of
Economic Interests, that payment must be aggregated with any other
gifts from the donor of the travel payment in order to determine
whether the donor is an economic interest of the official pursuant
to Section 87103(e).

Regulation 18728 sets forth a limited exception to this
general rule for intrastate travel provided directly in connection
with a speech. Such intrastate travel is not a payment and,
therefore, not a gift. Please be advised, however, that the
Commission is considering new gift and honoraria regulations which
may change this advice.

If you have further questions in connection with this issue,
please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Susan Bobrow
    Counsel, Legal Division

SH:SLB:ken
